name: Put Options 9/11
description:
The "Put Options 9/11" conspiracy theory alleges that individuals with prior knowledge of the September 11th attacks profited by trading in put options on the stocks of companies that were negatively impacted by the event, particularly airlines and financial institutions. Proponents claim that unusually high volumes of put options were purchased in the days leading up to the attacks, suggesting insider trading. The theory posits that these trades represent evidence of foreknowledge and complicity in the attacks. Key aspects include analysis of trading data, investigation into specific firms involved in the options trading, and attempts to identify the individuals who placed the trades.

The mainstream perspective, based on investigations by the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the 9/11 Commission, acknowledges unusual trading patterns but attributes them to a variety of factors, including coincidence, general market speculation, and the difficulty of definitively tracing the origins of all trades. These investigations found no conclusive evidence linking the put option trading to individuals with prior knowledge of the attacks. The 9/11 Commission Report addressed the issue, stating that while some suspicious trades occurred, they were not substantial enough to significantly impact the market and that the ultimate beneficiaries of these trades were often untraceable or unconnected to the attacks. Critics of the theory point out that the volume of suspicious trades was relatively small compared to overall market activity and that attributing them solely to insider knowledge is speculative.
subtitle: Alleged insider trading before 9/11.

claims.claim: Unusual put option activity proves foreknowledge of 9/11.
claims.evidence: Spikes in put option purchases for airlines and financial firms before 9/11.
claims.criticism: SEC and 9/11 Commission found no conclusive evidence linking these trades to foreknowledge. Market speculation and difficulty tracing trades are alternative explanations.

claims.claim: Specific firms or individuals profited significantly from these trades.
claims.evidence: Analysis of trading data suggesting large profits made on put options.
claims.criticism: Investigations found no direct links between those who profited and the 9/11 attacks. Profits were often relatively small in the context of the overall market.

claims.claim: Government investigations deliberately concealed the truth about the put options.
claims.evidence: Lack of transparency in investigation reports and alleged suppression of evidence.
claims.criticism: Government investigations were thorough but faced challenges in tracing the complex web of financial transactions. Lack of evidence is not necessarily proof of a cover-up.

keyFigures.name: Michael Ruppert
keyFigures.role: Early proponent of the put options theory.

keyFigures.name: 9/11 Commission
keyFigures.role: Investigated the put options trading and concluded there was no direct link to foreknowledge.

timeline.event: Initial reports and investigations into unusual put option activity.
9/11 Commission Report released, addressing the put options issue.
